items.nearby_content:
Abstract
Infrastructure  projects,  including  road  construction  projects,  inherently  operate  under  risky  and  hazardous  situations,  particularly  during  processes  involving  earthworks, temporary structures, heavy machinery and live traffic exposure. Such  situations will significantly increase the risk of occupational accidents and public  safety  incidents,  which  require  effective  safety  culture  and  risk  mitigation  strategies. This study aimed to identify and prioritise the critical factors influencing  safety culture in road construction projects and to evaluate suitable mitigation  strategies for reducing safety risks. A quantitative survey was conducted among  professionals involved in road construction projects in Malaysia, involving 36 valid  respondents  comprising  engineers,  contractors,  project  managers,  and  safety  practitioners. Relative weight of the safety criteria, sub-criteria, and the respective  mitigation strategies were established using the Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP).  Results showed that the economic and financial aspects were the most important  criterion, with an importance weight of 25.77%. This was followed by project risk

items.label: Malay Abstract (Abstrak)
items.notes: Abstrak confirmed by both DOC and PDF detection.
items.nearby_content:
Projek infrastruktur, termasuk projek pembinaan jalan, sememangnya beroperasi  dalam keadaan yang berbahaya, terutamanya semasa kerja-kerja melibatkan kerja  tanah, struktur sementara, jentera berat dan kerja yang terdedah kepada aliran  trafik.  Situasi  berikut  secara  tidak  langsung  meningkatkan  risiko  kemalangan  pekerjaan dan keselamatan awam, di mana keberkesanan budaya keselamatan  dan strategi mitigasi risiko diperlukan. Kajian ini bertujuan untuk mengenal pasti  dan mengutamakan faktor-faktor kritikal yang mempengaruhi budaya keselamatan  dalam  projek  pembinaan  jalan  dan  untuk  menganalisis  strategi  mitigasi  yang  sesuai  untuk  mengurangkan  risiko  keselamatan.  Tinjauan  kuantitatif  dilakukan  dalam kalangan pakar yang terlibat dengan projek pembinaan jalan di Malaysia  yang melibatkan seramai 36 responden termasuklah jurutera, kontraktor, pengurus  projek dan pakar keselamatan. Pemberat relatif kriteria keselamatan, subkriteria  dan  strategi  mitigasi,  telah  diperoleh  dengan  menggunakan  Proses  Hierarki  Analitik (AHP). Hasil dapatan menunjukkan bahawa faktor ekonomi dan kewangan
78:1 (2016) 1–5 | www.jurnalteknologi.utm.my | eISSN 2180–3722 |

1.0 INTRODUCTION
The construction industry has always been considered
one  of  the  most  complicated  and  diversified  industries  because of the presence of several parties involved in the  process,  differences  in  contractual  relations,  transitory  project structures, and types of work such as buildings,  highways, bridges, tunnels, and infrastructure constructions  [1]. In the construction industry, road construction projects  contribute to mobility at the national level, improved logistics  performance, economic efficiency, connectivity of regions,  and  accessibility  of  public  utilities  [2].  With  growing  transportation demand, road construction remains a high- priority development activity for many countries, such as  Malaysia.
Road  construction  projects  are  highly  dynamic  and
operationally  challenging.  Unlike  fixed-location  building  projects, road works commonly extend across long route  corridors  and  involve  simultaneous  activities  such  as  earthworks,  pavemen

items.nearby_content:
3.1 Prioritisation of Principal Criteria and Sub-Factors
The  AHP  analysis  demonstrated  that  economic  and
financial factors were the most influential criterion affecting  safety culture in road construction projects, with an overall  weight of 25.77%. This was followed by project risk factors  (24.60%)  and  skills-related  factors  (17.20%).  The  remaining principal criteria were resource factors (17.06%)  and health factors (15.38%). These findings show that  financial  capability,  risk  exposure,  and  workforce  competency  are  the  major  factors  influencing  safety  performance in road construction environments.
The emphasis on economic and financial considerations
implies that the maintenance of a safety culture cannot be  achieved without adequate financial resources. In practical  terms,  construction  organisations  require  dedicated  budgets  for  training,  competent  supervision,  personal  protective equipment, inspections, traffic control systems,  and  emergency  preparedness.  In  many  infrastructure  projects, particularly under competitive tendering and cost- sensitive contracts, safety expenditure is often viewed as  an operational cost rather than a
